medicine, employee health, research, and advocacy for maintain -
ing a healthy workforce with maximal productivity and safety in 
the work environment. From a biopsychosocial perspective, health 
includes a person’s complete physical, mental, and social well-
being. A worker’s fitness is not defined as just the absence of 
disease but described in terms of bodily function and structure, 
activity limitations, and/or environmental factors. 62 The modern 
workplace can potentially entail any or all of the following ele -
ments: physical, chemical, biologic, social, recreational, spiritual, 
and psychological. Therefore the broad goals of occupational and 
environmental medicine are promoting workers’ health, improv-
ing the work environment for wellness, and developing a culture 
that supports safety. Such a “safety culture” should foster a 
positive social climate to enhance team productivity without 
injury at work and must be reflected in leadership direction, 
management priorities, employee attitudes, policies, procedures, 
and measurable reported outcomes. For example, during the 
COVID-19 pandemic, the spread of community illness was 
linked to workplace outbreaks, prompting widespread adapta -
tions in public health, employee safety, surveillance practices, and 
business policies.28
Brief History of Occupational Medicine
Hippocrates described lead poisoning in miners during the fourth 
century BCE, but the first book credited with formally introduc -
ing the specialty was an Italian text, De Morbis Artificum Diatriba 
(Diseases of Workers), by Dr. Bernardino Ramazzini in 1700. The 
Introduction
Occupational medicine and vocational rehabilitation are two dis-
tinct yet interrelated health care disciplines, with occupational 
medicine focusing on optimizing the health of the worker and 
vocational rehabilitation aiming to maximize work participation. 
Both incorporate a holistic, multimodal approach to identify and 
account for specific impairments, address activity limitations, and 
enable job participation. These disciplines accomplish this through 
policies, procedures, programs, or adapted interactions with the 
workplace environment from the level of the affected worker to 
the system at large. Through the lens of a physiatrist, these fields 
of medicine share a familiar focus on patient-centered care using 
an interprofessional team to promote wellness, prevent and treat 
injuries, restore meaningful function, and adapt the environment 
to the working individual. The goals of occupational medicine 
and vocational rehabilitation are to preserve productivity and job 
performance respectful of any limitations using prevention, pro -
tection, rehabilitative treatment, adaptive equipment, and com -
pensatory strategies.
Occupational Medicine
Occupational medicine (also known as occupational health, oc -
cupational and environmental medicine, and [formerly] indus -
trial medicine) has evolved to encompass the clinical practice  
of assessing, treating, and preventing health disorders arising from 
or in association with job performance. It is the intersection of 
7
Occupational Medicine and Vocational 
Rehabilitation
ADAM SABY, MAURO ZAPPATERRA, AND DIXIE ARAGAKI
• Workers’ compensation treatment guidelines ensure injured 
workers receive standardized evidence-based care that aims for 
optimal recovery and timely return to work while allowing flexi -
bility for individualized treatment when necessary.
• Effective return-to-work interventions for injured workers in-
volve early coordination of care, workplace modifications, ther-
apeutic education, and a multidisciplinary approach to support 
sustained workability and optimal recovery.
• Among a broad range of physical and psychological factors, 
employees who “hardly ever” enjoy their job were most likely to 
report a back injury.
• For conditions that require relative rest, time-based activity re -
strictions are easier for patients to follow and for employers to 
accommodate.
• When assessing causation of a work injury, use the employee’s 
occupational mechanism of action to differentiate occupational 
diagnoses from nonoccupational diagnoses.
SUMMARY PEARLS

world was experiencing the industrial revolution with new bursts 
of productivity and innovation. With the paradigm shift from 
small-scale craftsmanship to mass-production factories, society 
and culture were subject to many changes, some beneficial and 
others burdensome. Ramazzini was a pioneer in recommending 
periods of rest, exercise, posture awareness, and hygiene to prevent 
work-related illnesses. There was an international movement to 
protect workers through age limits, work hour restrictions, safer 
building conditions, and safety standards to reduce hazardous 
exposures and risks of injury. In Germany, Chancellor Otto von 
Bismarck proposed the first modern system of workers’ compen-
sation insurance in 1884 (Workers’ Accident Insurance) and ad -
vocated for an Old Age and Disability Insurance Law of 1889 
with coverage of all worker categories, including industrial, agrar-
ian, artisans, and servants.
The industrial revolution in the United States catalyzed in -
creased building and manufacturing but burdened workers with 
higher expectations on pace, hours, and overall productivity (all of 
which are risk factors for musculoskeletal injuries). In the early 
1900s, it was common for Americans to work 10-hour days, 6 days 
a week with little recourse to negotiate adequate compensation or 
safe working conditions. Unsurprisingly, work-related accidents 
and deaths were higher before the labor movement helped to bring 
advocacy and policies to protect workers. During the early 20th 
century, workers’ compensation insurance rose in popularity due to 
increased awareness of dangerous working conditions and the need 
to offset the devastating financial impacts of work-related injury or 
illness for workers and families. Workers’ compensation insurance 
is the oldest social insurance program in the United States, now 
mandated for most businesses in all states except T exas (where it is 
optional). It is commonly based on a no-fault system, meaning that 
an injured worker can secure cash benefits and medical care without 
having to demonstrate that the employer or another party was neg-
ligent or caused an on-the-job incident.49 Workers’ compensation 
insurance is a key deterrent in reducing workplace injury litigation. 
Over time, demand grew for physicians to help address the negative 
physical, environmental, and psychosocial impacts as well as evalu-
ate, treat, and rehabilitate workers at the earliest opportunity. The 
outdated narrow term industrial medicine, used before 1960, was 
replaced by occupational medicine to incorporate broader elements 
of public health, environmental exposures, toxicology, and a wide 
variety of vocational injuries and disorders. Notably, physiatrists 
possess the clinical skills, holistic viewpoint, and team-oriented 
perspective to be uniquely effective in this field with their additional 
capacity to focus on occupational rehabilitation.
Epidemiology of Work-Related Fatality,  
Injury, and Illness
Between 1912 and 2022, the rate of unintentional work deaths 
dropped from 21 to 3.7 per 100,000 American workers. A total 
of 5,486 fatal work injuries were recorded in 2022 according to 
the US Bureau of Labor Statistics.54 The highest number of work-
place fatalities in 2022 was transportation related (38%), and the 
second highest was in construction and extraction workers (19%). 
Occupations with the highest fatality rate (23.5 per 10,000 full-
time equivalent [FTE] workers) were in the farming, fishing, and 
forestry industries. Notably, unintentional injuries of all causes 
(occupational and nonoccupational) have been the third leading 
cause of death in the United States between 2018 and 2022, trail-
ing heart disease and cancer, followed by COVID-19, cerebrovas-
cular disease, and chronic lower respiratory diseases. 34 Climate 
change also contributed to increasing work-related fatalities in 
2022 due to temperature extremes, specifically environmental 
heat. Private industry employers reported 2.8 million nonfatal 
workplace injuries and illnesses in 2022 at a rate of 2.3 cases per 
100 FTE workers.55 The increase was driven by a modest rise in 
musculoskeletal injuries and a sizable increase in respiratory 
illnesses secondary to COVID-19.
The most common reason for missed work in 2021 and 2022 
was “exposure to harmful substances or environments,” a cate -
gory that includes cases of COVID-19. The next two most 
prevalent causes of injury and illness were “overexertion and 
bodily reaction” and “slips, trips and falls.” These top three cat -
egories accounted for more than 75% of all nonfatal injuries and 
illnesses leading to days away from work. 38 Incidents related to 
overexertion involve lifting, carrying, throwing, and twisting 
type motions and can lead to work-related musculoskeletal dis -
orders. The  National Safety Council has focused on the dangers 
that fatigue brings to the work environment because research 
reveals that workers with sleep disorders are 1.6 times more 
likely to be injured, and 13% of job-related injuries are attrib -
uted to workers with fatigue. 32,46 Fatigue also impacts driver 
safety. Nonfatal occupational injuries are common in nursing 
and residential care facilities, veterinary services, police and fire 
protection services, manufacturing, and construction. Musculo -
skeletal sprains/strains and pain/soreness are the most reported 
conditions. However, other common occupational ailments are 
related to toxin exposure (e.g., pesticides, lead), hearing loss, 
heat/cold/radiation exposure, mental illness, cancer (up to 5%–
10% of malignancies are considered work related), infections, 
and respiratory diseases (e.g., an estimated 15% of new-onset 
asthma is work related). 30
Injury incidence differs by sex, age, and race. Males com -
prise most occupational fatalities. Whereas most fatal work 
injuries occurred in groups between 25 and 65 years old, 14% 
of the fatally injured workers in 2022 were age 65 years or 
older.54 Workers who identified as White or Hispanic/Latino 
had higher rates of death compared to Black, Asian, or 
Indigenous workers. 54 In addition to loss of life and livelihood, 
workplace injuries and illnesses include direct and indirect 
costs. Direct costs include workers’ compensation payments, 
medical expenses, and costs for legal services. Indirect costs 
include training replacement employees, accident investigation, 
implementation of corrective measures, lost productivity, re -
pairs of damaged equipment/property, lower employee morale, 
and absenteeism.
Safety in the Workplace
Safety Regulation
The Department of Labor (DOL) addresses the millions of inju -
ries and work-related deaths in the United States each year. Under 
the DOL, the Occupational Safety and Health Administration 
(OSHA) is the regulatory body tasked with creating and enforc -
ing occupational safety standards for private and public sector 
employers nationwide. OSHA’s general duty clause states each 
employer shall  provide “a place of employment which are free 
from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause 
death or serious physical harm to his employees.” The National 
Institute for  Occupational Safety (NIOSH) is a research body 
tasked with creating guidance for enforcement agencies, such as 
OSHA, on how to best prevent and address occupational injury.

SECTION 1  Evaluation
T o monitor safety, OSHA requires employers to keep records 
of all work-related reportable injuries, including any injury need -
ing medical management beyond first aid (e.g., x-rays, prescrip -
tion medications). OSHA also conducts planned inspections for 
hazardous industries or when there is a specific event, such as an 
occupational catastrophe, severe injury (e.g., work-related fatal -
ity), employee complaint, or spike in reportable injuries. T o im -
prove safety, OSHA provides training for public and private sector 
safety officials on how to comply with safety regulations.
Safety Strategies
Safety specialists, including hired consultants or those housed 
within a company’s environmental health and safety (EH&S) de-
partment, help employers adhere to state and federal regulations. 
Often accredited by the Board of Certified Safety Professionals, 
these specialists develop strategic plans to lower an employer’s 
injuries as compared with industry standards. Examples of success-
ful injury prevention programs featured in an OSHA White Paper 
show top-level leadership, employee participation, and a “find and 
fix” strategy are effective.
41
The hierarchy of hazard control is an organizational system 
used by OSHA and safety specialists to detail the stepwise ap-
proach in implementing safety measures. The five tiers of inter -
vention proposed from most to least effective are elimination, 
substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and 
personal protective equipment (Fig. 7.1).
37 Elimination and sub-
stitution are concerned with removing or replacing hazards, but 
they are best instituted when first designing the work project. 
When these top-level strategies are not feasible, engineering con -
trols help to isolate workers from the hazard. For example, a labo-
ratory biosafety cabinet with unidirectional airflow can separate 
workers from hazardous gases or liquids, especially in the event of 
a spill. The next safety tier is administrative controls, which 
 involve changes in workplace practice and policies. Examples 
 include reducing the time spent with hazardous materials by ro -
tating workers through workstations or educating workers to 
recognize early symptoms of occupational injury. The last and 
least effective tier of safety is personal protective equipment 
(PPE). PPE provides an immediate wearable barrier from the 
hazard and includes respirators, ear plugs, safety goggles, hard 
hats, flame-retardant aprons, bulletproof vests, and gloves.
Ergonomics is “the science of fitting workplace conditions and 
job demands to the capability of the working population.”
36 The 
goal of ergonomics is to prevent injuries and disorders associated 
with uncomfortable postures or cumulative trauma from repeated 
tasks. A workplace ergonomics program aims to reduce work-
related musculoskeletal disorders by mitigating awkward posi -
tioning, repetition, significant force, mechanical compression, 
vibration, temperature extremes, glare, inadequate lighting, and 
duration of exposure. Examples of ergonomic interventions 
include reducing the immediate physical effort to transport mate-
rials (e.g., via mechanical lift) or reducing the repetitive physical 
effort while using a workstation (e.g., via computer desktop 
ergonomics) (Box 7.1; Fig. 7.2).
40,45
Workers With a Disability
Preexisting Disability
The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) of 1990 was estab -
lished to provide greater employment opportunities to those with 
disabilities by preventing discrimination based on a physical or 
mental impairment. The ADA, as enforced by the Equal Employ-
ment Opportunity Commission, requires antidiscrimination 
measures be followed by all public and private sector employers 
with 15 or more employees.
47 The ADA prohibits employers from 
asking applicants medical questions during the interview process 
or prior to making a job offer. Once the applicant accepts an offer, 
the employer may require a preplacement physical to assess if the 
new employee can perform the job’s essential functions. A pre -
placement physical yields valuable information about a new em -
ployee’s ability to accomplish essential job tasks. Employees with 
a mismatch between their physical capabilities and their job tasks

Preplacement and Medical Surveillance Exams
Physical medicine and rehabilitation (PM&R) physicians can help 
prevent injuries in the workplace and accommodate employees 
with disabilities through the preplacement physical exam. The pri-
mary goal of the preplacement physical is to assess if the employee 
can safely perform the job’s essential functions. These essential func-
tions may include lifting objects up to a maximum weight (e.g., 
50 pounds) or identifying traffic light colors when driving (e.g., red 
vs. green). As a result, the physician must focus the history and 
physical to assess the employee’s ability to accomplish specific work 
tasks. If the employee is unable to complete portions of the exam, 
the physician must communicate these limitations to the employer.
In addition to the physical assessment, the physician investi -
gates for preexisting disabilities and the need for specific work 
restrictions. Certain medical diagnoses, such as severe pulmonary 
hypertension, may require clarification from the employee’s pri-
mary care physician about the need for medical restrictions (e.g., 
limit prolonged walking). If the medical restrictions for a preexist-
ing disability do not interfere with the job’s essential functions, 
the physician relays the information so the employer can provide 
reasonable accommodations.
A third component of the preplacement physical is identifying 
opportunities for occupational injury prevention. Occupations can 
expose employees to endemic diseases (e.g., malaria for researchers 
traveling to Tanzania) or have unique exposures (e.g., human blood 
exposures in a hospital setting). In many cases, physicians can mini-
mize occupational hazards by offering chemoprophylaxis (e.g., anti-
malarials) or vaccination (e.g., hepatitis B vaccination) during the 
preplacement physical. Hazards that require regular vaccine boosters 
(e.g., rabies) or screening (e.g., tuberculosis in a K-12 school setting) 
can spur the creation of a routine medical surveillance program.
A medical surveillance program involves recurring health as -
sessments to prevent and monitor for occupational injury. These 
assessments may involve a health questionnaire to review changes 
to the employee’s medical status (e.g., new immunocompromised 
condition) or job roles (e.g., a pharmacist takes on a new role that 
requires compounding chemotherapeutic medications). The as -
sessment may require a focused physical exam and diagnostic 
studies to screen for early signs of illness (e.g., annual chest x-rays 
for asbestosis in construction workers). The Department of T rans-
portation (DOT) medical exam is a nationwide example of a 
medical surveillance program that assesses the health of employees 
who drive commercial motor vehicles. The DOT exam monitors 
for various health conditions, including those that may cause sud-
den incapacity (e.g., epilepsy), which may affect the safe operation 
of a commercial motor  v ehicle.
Occupational Injury and Illness
Despite best efforts to promote occupational safety, injuries occur. 
Under the workers’ compensation insurance system, an employee 
fills out a claim form explaining the injurious episode. The em -
ployee notes the date and mechanism of injury as well as the in -
jured body parts (e.g., left shoulder, low back, left knee). At this 
point, the physician obtains a more detailed medical history and 
performs a physical exam. As part of the assessment and plan, the 
physician must determine causation of the injury as occupational 
versus nonoccupational.

SECTION 1  Evaluation
A basic standard is that to be considered occupational and eli -
gible for workers’ compensation benefits, the injury must arise out 
of employment (AOE) and occur during the course of employ -
ment (COE). In other words, the injury must have occurred on 
the job and within the scope of the job’s duties. For example, a 
mail carrier who falls on the sidewalk while delivering mail for the 
employer meets AOE/COE criteria. On the other hand, a mail 
carrier who falls on the sidewalk while visiting a restaurant during 
a lunchbreak does not meet AOE/COE and, as a result, is not 
eligible for workers’ compensation benefits.
While insurance claims adjustors can help determine AOE/
COE, only a physician can determine which diagnoses are occu -
pational versus nonoccupational. This process, called apportion -
ment, helps determine what diagnoses are covered for treatment 
in the occupational medicine setting. A PM&R physician has a 
unique skill set in determining apportionment because of famil -
iarity with the biomechanics of trauma. The employee’s mecha -
nism of injury should directly cause the occupational diagnosis. 
Other diagnoses not directly caused by the cited mechanism of 
injury are considered preexisting, whether known or unknown to 
the employee. For example, a school janitor sweeping the floors 
hits the anterior aspect of the knee against a classroom desk. After 
a thorough history and physical, the employee’s diagnoses include 
a patellar contusion and tibiofemoral osteoarthritis. By reviewing 
the mechanism of injury cited (i.e., a low-amplitude blunt trauma 
to the anterior knee), the PM&R physician assesses that the patel-
lar contusion is occupational, but the tibiofemoral osteoarthritis 
is a preexisting nonoccupational condition. As a result, workers’ 
compensation benefits cover treatment for the occupational diag-
noses (e.g., patellar contusion) while preexisting diagnoses (e.g., 
osteoarthritis) are deferred to the employee’s primary care physi-
cian for management.
A common series of injuries in the workplace are cumulative 
trauma disorders. The mechanism of injury cited typically in -
cludes weeks to months of repetitive work tasks. One of the most 
common cumulative trauma disorders, often resulting from re -
petitive keyboarding and mousing at computer workstations, is 
carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS).14 A pooled study of workers from 
various industries found that peak hand force and time spent 
performing forceful hand exertion are associated with an in -
creased risk of CTS.20 Though increased computer mouse use and 
exposure to significant vibration (e.g., operating a jackhammer) 
have been implicated as risks,1 there are multiple nonoccupational 
factors that also predict CTS, including obesity, pregnancy, smok-
ing, and older age. 33 While ergonomics can minimize physical 
discomfort with equipment and workstation design, addressing 
relevant nonoccupational conditions is necessary for healing. 53
Determining Temporary Disability
In addition to diagnosing and treating an employee’s occupational 
condition, a PM&R physician has a key role in creating the em -
ployee’s activity prescription (i.e., Activity Rx). Through an un -
derstanding of neuromusculoskeletal physiology, the physician 
writes a set of biomechanical restrictions an employee can follow 
(e.g., limit walking for an ankle sprain) to ensure optimal healing. 
Successful biomechanical restrictions can be applied to the patient 
in any setting, including work, home, during travel, and more. As 
a result, a range of activities the patient can safely partake in is also 
implied (e.g., answering phone calls or using a computer). An 
effective Activity Rx prevents unnecessary total disability and 
promotes functional independence.
Time-based restrictions (e.g., limit walking to 15 minutes per 
hour) are especially useful when a medical condition requires 
relative rest as opposed to total rest. Though granting an employee 
time off from work (i.e., temporary total disability) can create 
patient satisfaction, it creates an unrealistic expectation about 
healing as an all-or-none phenomenon. Setting expectations 
about advancing activity to coincide with rehabilitation treatment 
helps employees achieve their functional goals in a stepwise 
 process.
Along with specifying restrictions, an Activity Rx should be 
simple enough for the employee to follow and for the employer 
to accommodate. With information in the Activity Rx, a voca -
tional rehabilitation or disability management specialist helps to 
return the employee to a suitable working environment. The 
objective is limiting the employee’s temporary total disability 
because the longer the employee is out of work, the more likely 
the employee will not return to productive employment. 11 Also, 
early mobility with multiple musculoskeletal conditions en -
hances recovery. For example, workers with subacute low back 
pain who received a recommendation to “stay active” returned to 
work significantly earlier than those who received standard pri -
mary medical care. 32
A physician should learn from the employer’s disability special-
ist about what specific activity restrictions hinder or help the 
employer’s ability to accommodate. The best accommodation is 
keeping an employee in the same working environment by place-
ment on a part-time or rotational work status. If this is not fea -
sible, the next form of accommodation is finding alternative 
work, sometimes in a different department, to keep the employee 
tied to the general workplace. Vocational rehabilitation methods 
using graded work conditioning and work hardening increase the 
likelihood of returning to full employment.48
Treating Temporary Disability
Work-related musculoskeletal disorders are the most common oc-
cupational medicine conditions. Of those, low back injuries are 
the leading cause of workplace disorders and lost workdays. 22 
Back pain is strongly influenced by an employee’s self-perception 
of having poor general health and feeling exhausted. 18 While not 
the cause of an employee’s diagnosis, an employee’s perception of 
work has a strong influence on prognosis. A pivotal study evaluat-
ing over 3,000 Boeing aircraft employees found the strongest 
predictor for filing a low back workers’ compensation claim was 
low job satisfaction. 3 Compared to patients without workers’ 
compensation claims, those with claims are more likely to have 
worse functional status and receive disability compensation at 
4 years postinjury. 2 Other factors that prolong disability include 
psychological issues, such as depressive symptoms, catastrophiz -
ing, and low self-confidence.42
As such, interventions to prevent and treat disability must fo -
cus on measures beyond tissue pathology. A holistic approach that 
addresses a patient’s overall mental and physical health leads to 
better disability outcomes. Return to work is based on biopsycho-
social elements such as recovery beliefs and pain behaviors. The 
patient’s expectation of recovery is one of the most consistent 
predictors of returning to work from any health condition. 7 On 
the other hand, unrealistic treatment expectations, such as those 
with surgical outcomes in low back pain, cause decreased satisfac-
tion, reduced treatment compliance, and lower return to work.7,43 
Managing recovery expectations with a patient helps identify 
those at risk for delayed recovery.

119Chapter 7 Occupational Medicine and Vocational Rehabilitation
Work Is Therapy
The ability to work is a major predictor of a person’s self-worth 
and provides people with a sense of purpose. An individual’s em-
ployment status is not only meaningful to one’s financial security 
but is also linked to an individual’s long-term health. It has been 
shown that there is a strong relationship between unemployment 
and all-cause mortality, including suicide and cardiovascular dis -
ease.25,39 Individuals who are unable to return to work because of 
a disability may experience increased physical pain, decondition -
ing, anxiety, depression, social isolation, and financial hardship. 
T otal disability decreases quality of life and increases health care 
utilization. Within limits, working is therapeutic. A PM&R phy-
sician who understands how employment enhances well-being 
can better align a patient’s expectations with rehabilitation goals. 
A systematic review assessing lumbar surgery for radiculopathy 
found patients were more likely to return to work when they were 
working preoperatively or had shorter duration of preoperative 
symptoms.26 Furthermore, longer preoperative disability and de -
pression predicted longer postoperative disability.
Determining Permanent Disability
Employees reach maximum medical improvement (MMI) when 
they have received the standard medical workup and treatments 
used to manage their occupational condition. At this point, fur -
ther treatment is not expected to have a significant impact on 
functional status. As a result, if employees cannot return to regu -
lar duty, their temporary partial disability is assessed as a perma -
nent partial disability. In conversation with patients, the physician 
determines the restrictions for permanent partial disability to 
maximize patients’ function in the workplace while minimizing 
flareups of their condition.
Once a physician dictates permanent restrictions, the employer 
must engage the employee in the interactive process to see if long-
term accommodations can be made. Often, the ability to accom -
modate depends on the severity of the restriction, such that mild 
limitations (e.g., limit lifting to 50 pounds) are more likely to be 
accommodated by the employer than severe limitations (e.g., 
limit lifting to 5 pounds). If the permanent restrictions prevent an 
employee from completing essential job functions, then the em -
ployer can legally terminate the person’s employment through 
medical separation. In this case, the employee may be eligible to 
receive educational funds for training in alternate occupations.
T o better include workers with a permanent partial disability 
in the US workforce, there are federal regulations and incentives 
for employers. Section 503 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 
prohibits federal contractors and subcontractors from discrimi -
nating against individuals with disabilities (IWDs). It also re -
quires employers to take affirmative action to recruit, hire, and 
retain IWDs to make up 7% of their hired workforce. The ADA 
Amendments Act of 2008 provides job opportunities for IWDs 
and updates nondiscrimination provisions. 52 Employers hiring 
workers with a new disability on vocational rehabilitation can be 
eligible for annual tax credits.57
There are several agencies that provide vocational resources to 
IWDs. The federal government sponsors programs such as the 
Employer Assistance and Resource Network (EARN), 12 which 
helps IWDs to pursue careers. The Veteran’s Administration (VA) 
provides a well-known vocational rehabilitation program to veter-
ans with service-connected disabilities. The VA vocational pro -
gram, which boasts an 89% satisfaction rate among participants, 
offers job placement assistance, advises veterans on small business 
ventures, and provides apprenticeship opportunities.56 Moreover, 
veterans who completed the program earned on average $18,000 
more in annual income than participants who did not. USAJobs 
is the federal government’s official employment site that posts 
noncompetitive appointments for its agencies, such as the 
National  Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), to 
employees with disabilities.58 Nonprofit organizations, such as the 
National Industries for the Blind, also help individuals with 
specific disabilities achieve employment.35
Vocational Rehabilitation
Vocational rehabilitation is a multidisciplinary approach to opti -
mize work participation for employees with health-related impair-
ments, limitations, or restrictions. 13 Vocational rehabilitation 
benefits individuals who have a health condition, injury, illness, 
or disability that may impact their work participation. Vocational 
rehabilitation practitioners practice the integrative biopsychoso -
cial approach of the World Health Organization’s International 
Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health  (ICF) and as -
sess the variety of factors that can lead to work disability at the 
individual and societal levels. Vocational rehabilitation can im -
prove return to work, increase functioning, and reduce pain. 29 
Specific interventions can be used to address various impairments: 
Exercise can address physical disability, cognitive behavioral ther-
apy can address psychosocial issues, graduated work simulation 
activities can assist job training, and supportive counseling can 
address labor, employer, insurance, and social issues.
Biopsychosocial Approach to Work Disability
Given today’s demand for worker productivity and workplace 
wellness, vocational rehabilitation is a key mitigating measure for 
workplace disability. From a global perspective, Article 23 of the 
United Nation’s Universal Declaration of Human Rights specifies 
that all people have the right to work, and the International Labor 
Organization ensures that all people have decent work. 24,61  
A common message from these international bodies is that an 
emphasis on overall health can be facilitated by enhancing an in -
dividual’s ability and capacity to work. When work disability 
arises, it is key that appropriate steps are taken to mitigate its 
negative effects on workers, their families, and society.
Vocational rehabilitation programs aim to improve productiv-
ity of individual workers and their sense of social security, 
 well-being, and personal fulfillment. Vocational rehabilitation 
programs are most effective when they utilize a holistic worker-
centered approach.15 This approach is based on a biopsychosocial 
model, such as the ICF (Fig. 7.3), where a particular health condi-
tion, injury, or illness can lead to work disability. The ICF is a 
comprehensive framework for organizing information on func -
tioning and disability. It conceptualizes functioning as a “dynamic 
interaction between a person’s health condition, environmental 
factors, and personal factors.” The ICF integrates both the 
 medical and social models into a biopsychosocial synthesis. This 
approach acknowledges the roles of both health conditions and 
environmental factors in disability.
This biopsychosocial model is particularly effective for under -
standing work disability. A particular health condition, injury, or 
illness can lead to work disability, which may be associated with 
physical, mental, cognitive, sensory (vision and hearing), emo -
tional, or psychological health conditions. Work disability can

manifest as an impairment of body function (e.g., weakness) and 
body structure (e.g., muscle), limitation of activity (e.g., limited 
lifting ability), and/or restriction of participation (e.g., restricted 
employment). The level and extent of work disability is influenced 
by contextual factors such as the environment (e.g., workplace 
support and accommodation) and personal factors (e.g., motiva -
tion and coping). The ICF’s inclusive approach recognizes various 
forms of work, including paid jobs and volunteer roles with com-
parable tasks.
The ICF employs a biopsychosocial approach to describe func-
tioning and disability as umbrella terms that encompass the posi-
tive and negative aspects of health from the multiple biologic, 
individual, and social perspectives. The ICF framework is divided 
into two main parts: (1) functioning and disability and (2) con -
textual factors. Each part is further subdivided as follows:
 1. F unctioning and disability
• Body functions and str uctures
• A ctivities and participation
 2. Contextual factors
• Environmental
• Personal
The ICF (see Fig. 7.3) highlights that functioning and disabil-
ity are outcomes of interactions between health conditions 
( diseases, disor ders, injuries) and contextual factors. Human func-
tioning is classified at three levels: body or body part, the whole 
person, and the whole person in a social context. Disability in -
volves dysfunction at one or more of these levels: impairments, 
activity limitations, and participation restrictions. Contextual 
factors include external environmental factors (e.g., social atti -
tudes, architectural characteristics, legal and social structures, cli -
mate, and terrain) as well as internal personal factors (e.g., sex, 
age, coping styles, social background, education, profession, past 
and current experiences, overall behavior patterns, and character).

and Evaluation
A comprehensive vocational assessment is critical in the develop -
ment of a vocation-focused plan of care. Comprehensive assessments

involve the description and evaluation of nonmodifiable aspects 
(e.g., sociodemographic), health condition–related factors, environ-
mental factors, functional abilities, and restrictions. All these com-
ponents are vital to determine and optimize work-related capacities 
for part-time, full-time, light, modified duty, or self-employment 
settings. Currently, two major approaches to assess work functioning 
exist: patient- r eported instruments and clinician-administered test-
ing of  abilities. Both appr oaches, with their respective advantages 
and disadvantages, complement each other to provide a whole 
picture of the worker.
Objective tests are standardized to measure tangible outcomes 
and are recognized by legal bodies but are usually labor intensive. 
One common example of objective testing is the functional capacity 
evaluation (FCE). Conversely, information from self-reported in-
struments is easy and fast to obtain but reflects the subjective evalu-
ation of the patient, including perceived environmental barriers. In 
addition, the patient requires an appropriate literacy level for proper 
responding. An example of a questionnaire that is used in the voca-
tional rehabilitation setting is the Work  R ehabilitation Question-
naire (WORQ).15 One advantage of WORQ is that it was developed 
based on the ICF model and intended for generic needs in voca -
tional rehabilitation. Despite differences between the patient-
reported and clinician-administered testing, recent studies conclude 
that integrating the two approaches might complement each other 
in terms of time, costs, and information gained.
Another aspect that is essential to a vocational rehabilitation as-
sessment is determining the educational needs of the worker and the 
type of job in which the worker will be engaging or reengaging. 
Having such knowledge will help guide the vocational rehabilitation 
practitioner in managing or referring services that would develop 
and/or enhance skills essential to employment, such as reading, writ-
ing, and problem solving. Investigating the relationship between the 
worker and the employer reveals employment potential and support 
of the employer for any workplace accommodation.
Workers’ Compensation Guidelines
Workers’ compensation treatment guidelines are established to 
ensure injured workers receive appropriate and effective medical

care following a work-related injury or illness. These guidelines 
are developed based on evidence-based medicine, best practices, 
and input from health care professionals. They aim to standardize 
treatment protocols to achieve optimal outcomes for injured 
workers. T reatment guidelines are typically based on best practices 
in the field of occupational medicine and rehabilitation. They 
may be regularly updated to reflect advances in medical knowl -
edge and changes in treatment standards. While specific treat -
ment guidelines can vary depending on the jurisdiction and the 
nature of the injury, there are some common elements: evaluation 
and diagnosis, treatment modalities recommended, duration and 
frequency of treatment, criteria for referral, return-to-work rec -
ommendations, follow-up, monitoring, and reassessment.
T reatment guidelines often outline the process for evaluating 
and diagnosing work-related injuries or illnesses. This may involve 
initial assessments by health care providers, diagnostic tests (e.g., 
x-rays, magnetic resonance imaging), and consultations with spe -
cialists as needed to determine the extent and nature of the injury. 
They often include recommendations for facilitating the injured 
worker’s return to work in a safe and timely manner and expected 
days off from work. This may involve gradual increases in work 
activities, modifications to job duties or work environments, and 
coordination with employers to ensure a smooth transition back 
to work.
The guidelines also specify the types of treatments and inter -
ventions that are considered appropriate. This may include 
 medications, physical therapy, chiropractic care, acupuncture, 
occupational therapy, and other rehabilitative services aimed at 
relieving pain, restoring function, and facilitating recovery. The 
guidelines help to provide recommendations regarding the dura -
tion and frequency of various interventions for diverse types of 
injuries. This helps ensure that injured workers receive timely and 
appropriate care without unnecessary delays or overutilization of 
services.
Finally, the guidelines typically emphasize the importance of 
ongoing monitoring and reassessment of the injured worker’s 
progress throughout the treatment process. This allows health care 
providers to adjust treatment plans as needed based on the indi -
vidual’s response to treatment and any changes in the condition. 
By following workers’ compensation treatment guidelines, health 
care providers can ensure that injured workers receive appropriate 
and effective care focused on restoring function, improving out -
comes, and promoting a timely return to work. These guidelines 
ensure consistency and fairness in the provision of medical ser -
vices to injured workers across different health care settings.
Using guidelines to authorize treatment in workers’ compensa-
tion cases is crucial for several reasons. First, treatment guidelines 
provide standardized protocols for evaluating and managing 
work-related injuries or illnesses, ensuring consistency and unifor-
mity in the care provided to injured workers, regardless of the 
health care setting or provider involved. Second, these guidelines 
are typically based on current medical evidence and best practices 
in the field, ensuring that injured workers receive treatments 
 supported by scientific research and likely to produce the best 
outcomes. Third, treatment guidelines often include recommen -
dations for cost-effective interventions that prioritize the efficient 
use of resources while delivering high-quality care and helping 
payers contain costs associated with workers’ compensation 
claims. Additionally, guidelines provide clear criteria for deter -
mining when specific treatments are medically necessary and ap -
propriate, streamlining the authorization process, reducing delays, 
and ensuring timely access to necessary care. They help ensure the 
quality and appropriateness of care by establishing benchmarks 
and standards for evaluating treatment outcomes, allowing health 
care providers to demonstrate that they meet established stan -
dards of quality and effectiveness. In many jurisdictions, treat -
ment guidelines allow payers to demonstrate they are fulfilling 
their legal obligations to provide appropriate medical benefits to 
injured workers.
Typically, physicians can use state guidelines to recommend 
treatment for injured workers. If state guidelines do not exist, 
then national guidelines, such as those found through the 
 American College of Occupational and Environmental Medicine 
or Official Disability Guidelines (ODG), can be used. While ad -
hering to treatment guidelines in workers’ compensation cases is 
beneficial, there are situations where deviation from these guide -
lines may be necessary or even appropriate. T reatment guidelines 
may not fully account for the unique characteristics and needs of 
individual patients. Deviating from guidelines allows health care 
providers to tailor treatment plans to meet the specific circum -
stances of each injured worker, considering factors such as age, 
comorbidities, psychosocial factors, and preferences. Deviation 
from guidelines may be justified when there is compelling evi -
dence to support alternative approaches not yet incorporated into 
the guidelines. Complex cases involving multiple comorbidities 
may also require deviation from guidelines to develop a compre -
hensive and individualized treatment plan. Furthermore, patients 
may have preferences or values that differ from the recommenda-
tions outlined in treatment guidelines, and health care providers 
should engage patients in shared decision making and respect 
their autonomy in determining their treatment preferences. In 
certain situations, deviation from treatment guidelines may be 
justified if the  physician can demonstrate the alternative approach 
is medically necessary and reasonable under the circumstances. 
Health care providers should document their rationale to mitigate 
potential legal risks. While deviation from treatment guidelines 
should not be taken lightly, it is essential to recognize that guide-
lines serve as general recommendations rather than rigid man -
dates. Health care providers must balance adherence to guidelines 
with the need to provide individualized, patient-centered care that 
optimizes outcomes and respects patients’ preferences and values.
Return-to-Work Interventions
Return to work following musculoskeletal injuries is a complex 
topic, and the outcomes are variable. Return to work is not solely 
dependent on the degree of injury and physical ability but is 
 influenced by a range of patient-related factors and external cir -
cumstances that are all within the four domains of the ICF frame-
work. Similarly, the outcomes for returning to work can range 
from returning to the previous job with no modifications, return-
ing to the previous job with modifications, returning to the same 
workplace in a different capacity, or finding a new job altogether. 
The goal is for the injured worker to heal and be able to sustain 
work after the injury occurs, with the ideal outcome of lasting 
ability to work.
Early intervention with coordination of care and rehabilitation 
is integral to leading a person back to working life after an injury 
or illness. This may include workplace modifications, therapeutic 
education, increasing physical fitness, work conditioning, 
decreasing anxiety or depression, managing stress, and increasing 
self- efficacy.4,8 A review determined that the top five interventions 
for patients with occupational injuries  include (1) acquiring skills; 
(2) health services, systems, and  policies; (3) products and

Another recent systematic review aimed to determine the 
 current state of therapeutic methods used in rehabilitation for 
individuals with back pain and to identify the most effective 
therapy for enhancing or restoring workability. Interventions 
that incorporate a physical therapy approach with a significant 
emphasis on exercise supervised by trained coaches are  clinically 
effective in improving pain intensity, disability, and quality of 
life. In addition, a manual therapy program that includes stay-
active therapy, stretching, and manual manipulation improved 
return-to-work rates. Additionally, the social interaction between 
the workplace and the employees is a crucial factor in work-
related outcomes.27
When workers are injured, simple strategies of workplace in -
terventions, such as environmental modifications and schedule 
changes or modifications to duties and tasks, can help. In people 
with musculoskeletal disorders, workplace interventions reduced 
time to return to work and increased incidence of the return more 
than usual care. 59 Based on DOL data, employee accommoda -
tions are very cost effective, with over one-half of accommoda -
tions costing nothing to the employer.53
A review looking at effectiveness of workplace interventions 
found interventions primarily in three domains: health focused, 
service coordination, and work modification. The health-focused 
interventions included graded activity, exercise, cognitive behav -
ioral therapy, work hardening, and multicomponent interven -
tions (a combination of medical assessment, physical therapy, 
psychology, and occupational therapy). It has been shown the 
physical conditioning programs for people with back and neck 
pain that incorporate a cognitive behavioral approach reduce sick 
leave when compared to usual care. 46 The service coordination 
interventions included communication between health care pro -
viders and workplace, developing return-to-work plans, educa -
tion, and training. The work modification interventions involved 
modified duties, modified hours, and ergonomic or worksite 
changes. There is strong evidence that multidomain interventions 
from two of the three abovementioned domains reduced duration 
away from work, lowered costs, and improved work functioning.
For individual interventions, there is moderate evidence that 
work accommodations improved lost time from work. From a 
recent review, the most commonly reported accommodations 
 included modifying job responsibilities, changing workplace 
 policies, providing supportive personnel, implementing flexible 
scheduling, and offering assistive technology. 60 Therefore, for 
improved return to work, implementing graded activity with 
modified duties and implementing a multidomain intervention 
for patients with musculoskeletal and pain-related conditions can 
improve return to work and work functioning.8,50
The Institute for Work and Health, an independent nonprofit 
organization, has established seven principles for successful return 
to work, an evidence-based summary of the most effective work -
place interventions, which is intended for all workplaces. 23 The 
seven principles include (1) a workplace with a strong commit -
ment to health and safety demonstrated by behavior, (2) offer of 
modified work to injured/ill workers, (3) return-to-work planners 
to ensure the plan, (4) supervisors trained in return to work,  
(5) employers who make early and considerate contact with in -
jured/ill workers, (6) employers and health care providers who 
communicate with each other, and (7) the worker who has access 
to clinical interventions for the management of pain, disability, 
depression, and poor expectations for recovery.7
Work Hardening and Work Conditioning
When an employer does not have options for graded activity or 
modified work, other strategies for return to work are work con -
ditioning, work hardening programs, and functional restoration 
programs (FRPs). PM&R physicians can recommend these pro -
grams when there is evidence that the patient has plateaued with 
routine active physical therapy treatments, and there are physical 
deficits that the patient needs to meet job demands. Work condi-
tioning is an intensive course of physical therapy for the purpose 
of training toward a specific job requirement, such as repetitive 
tasks or increasing capacities for reaching, standing, walking, or 
lifting. For health care providers who are recommending work 
conditioning, it is important to document that the  patient has 
undergone physical therapy with benefits but has plateaued and 
has not been able to meet the work requirements mandated by the 
current position. Work conditioning can help with work-specific 
duties by focusing on building endurance.
Work hardening, which is more intensive than work condi -
tioning, is an individualized, interdisciplinary, job-specific 
 program to assist the injured worker to return to work. Work 
hardening is recommended when there is a physical, functional, 
behavioral, and/or vocational deficit that prevents the ability to 
achieve job demands. Work hardening uses real or simulated work 
activities and graded increase in activity to help restore function 
and vocational ability physically, socially, and psychologically. 
Participation in a work conditioning or hardening program does 
not preclude concurrently being at work.
Functional Restoration Programs
An FRP , unlike work conditioning and hardening programs, is an 
interdisciplinary treatment approach for individuals with disabling 
musculoskeletal conditions. The programs use the biopsychosocial 
model of rehabilitation, focusing on active conditioning activities, 
self-directed exercises, and psychosocial interventions using a team 
approach with a combination of medical doctors, physical thera -
pists, occupational therapists, psychologists, and vocational thera-
pists. The focus and emphasis are on functional improvement rather 
than elimination of pain. An FRP has shown to decrease sick leave 
with full-time return-to-work rates ranging from 57% to 80%.6,26,44
The physical demand level at discharge predicted return to 
work and work retention 1 year after completing an FRP . 16 
 Furthermore, the length of disability does not decrease the bene -
fits for returning to work or health care utilization. However, the 
use of an FRP early in the rehabilitative process is more cost effec-
tive when compared to receiving the treatment after the individ -
ual has exhausted all other treatments and as a treatment of last 
resort.51 The ODG state there are several criteria for admission 
into a functional restoration or chronic pain program. The criteria 
state that the patient has a chronic pain condition with loss of 
functional capacity that lasts longer than 3 months, that the pa -
tient has tried first-line treatment that has been unsuccessful, and 
that the claimant has undergone a  thorough multidisciplinary 
evaluation, which usually includes an evaluation by a medical

Once the multidisciplinary evaluation has taken place, the specific 
functional goals of the program are individualized to each patient. 
As the patient undergoes treatment, compliance with the program 
and subjective and objective functional gains are documented. An 
FRP is not to exceed 160 hours.
Functional Capacity Evaluation
The FCE is a multifaceted process designed to objectively assess 
an individual’s functional capacity for a return-to-work assess -
ment. FCEs exist for both job-specific functional assessment 
and overall assessment. 21 FCEs are recommended prior to and 
upon completion of an FRP , work conditioning, or work 
hardening program directed toward a specific job. FCEs can be 
valuable for determining return to work for a job with specific 
goals and requirements. 31 A group of 354 patients with chronic 
disabling musculoskeletal disorders underwent FCEs at both 
admission and discharge from their FRP . Among them, 96% 
improved their physical demand levels and 56% met the lifting 
requirements of their job, with strength gains approaching 50%. 
Improvement in FCE- determined physical demand was 
predictive of returning to work upon discharge and retaining 
employment 1 year later. 16
An FCE is more likely to produce meaningful results when 
the worker is properly motivated, collaborates with the examiner, 
and the testing has a specific, purposeful focus. Providing the 
evaluator with a job description from the employer enhances the 
FCE by allowing for a more accurate assessment of the worker’s 
abilities and capacities. Because an FCE evaluates a person’s work 
capacity to determine fitness for duty, it should not be used solely 
to assess a worker’s effort or compliance, assign permanent work 
restrictions, determine further treatment, predict reinjury, or 
establish MMI.17
There continues to be a lack of evidence regarding the reliabil-
ity and validity of the FCE and how performance correlates to 
real-life job tasks.10 Furthermore, there continues to be concerns 
with inconsistent measurements of endurance and effort. There -
fore more research is needed to increase the reliability and validity 
of the FCE.9
Summary
In light of the many individual and societal benefits of maintain -
ing a high employability rate, it is important to effectively imple -
ment strategies offered by occupational medicine and vocational 
rehabilitation teams. A primary focus should be prevention of 
work-related illnesses, injuries, and fatalities through thoughtful 
leadership and planning, protective policies, ergonomic design, 
and a health-conscious workforce. When preexisting or acquired 
injuries cause limitations in job activities, the multidisciplinary 
occupational medicine and vocational rehabilitative specialists can 
be called upon to help treat, compensate, restore, or optimize 
work participation and function.
